This is a general computer science question outside my role as Domain Authority for this project. I'm not going to shoehorn it into my domain analysis output format — that would be dishonest. But I can give you a straight technical answer.

---

## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — when insertions arrive in sorted order, the tree degenerates into a linked list.

```
INSERT(tree, key):
    node = tree.root
    parent = nil

    while node ≠ nil:
        parent = node
        if key < node.key:
            node = node.left
        else:
            node = node.right

    newNode = Node(key)
    newNode.parent = parent

    if parent = nil:
        tree.root = newNode        // tree was empty
    else if key < parent.key:
        parent.left = newNode
    else:
        parent.right = newNode
```

The depth of the tree determines cost. Without balancing, depth can reach **n**.

---

## Red-Black Tree: How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a BST with one extra bit per node (color: **red** or **black**) and five invariants:

| # | Property |
|---|----------|
| 1 | Every node is red or black |
| 2 | The root is black |
| 3 | Every nil leaf is black |
| 4 | A red node's children are both black (no two consecutive reds) |
| 5 | All paths from a node to its descendant nil leaves have the **same black-height** |

Property 5 is the key insight — it guarantees the longest path is at most **2× the shortest**, bounding tree height to **O(2 log(n+1))** → **O(log n)**.

### Insertion Fix-Up

After a standard BST insert (new node colored **red**), properties 2 or 4 may be violated. The fix-up handles three cases, mirrored for left/right symmetry:

```
RB-INSERT-FIXUP(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color = RED:
        if z.parent = z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            // Case 1: Uncle is RED → recolor
            if uncle.color = RED:
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                uncle.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent          // move problem up

            else:
                // Case 2: z is a right child → left rotate to reduce to Case 3
                if z = z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    LEFT-ROTATE(tree, z)

                // Case 3: z is a left child → recolor + right rotate
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                RIGHT-ROTATE(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // symmetric: swap left/right in all of the above

    tree.root.color = BLACK                  // enforce Property 2
```

### The Three Cases Explained

| Case | Condition | Action | Effect |
|------|-----------|--------|--------|
| **1** | Uncle is red | Recolor parent, uncle → black; grandparent → red; move z up | Pushes violation toward root |
| **2** | Uncle is black, z is inner child | Rotate z's parent (transforms into Case 3) | Straightens the path |
| **3** | Uncle is black, z is outer child | Recolor + rotate grandparent | **Terminates** — tree is fixed |

### Rotation (O(1) pointer surgery)

```
LEFT-ROTATE(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left
    if y.left ≠ nil:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent
    if x.parent = nil:
        tree.root = y
    else if x = x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x
    x.parent = y
```

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | BST (worst) | Red-Black Tree (worst) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Insert | O(n) | **O(log n)** |
| Search | O(n) | **O(log n)** |
| Delete | O(n) | **O(log n)** |
| Fix-up rotations | — | **≤ 2 per insert** |

The fix-up loop runs at most O(log n) iterations (Case 1 climbs the tree), but performs **at most 2 rotations** total before terminating via Case 3. That's why red-black trees are preferred in practice (e.g., `std::map`, Java `TreeMap`, Linux CFS scheduler) — predictable, bounded rebalancing cost.
